items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.

items.q: Which item is removed first from a stack?
items.why: Students confuse stacks (LIFO - like a stack of plates) with queues (FIFO - like a checkout line). The 'stack of plates' analogy makes LIFO concrete.

items.q: Why is UDP used for live video streaming despite being unreliable?
items.correct: Lower overhead and latency - retransmitting late packets would harm real-time quality.
items.misconception: Because TCP cannot handle video data.
items.random_wrong: Because UDP is always faster than TCP.
items.why: Students assume reliability is always desirable. For real-time media, a dropped packet is preferable to a delayed one - UDP trades reliability for timeliness, which suits live media.

items.q: Which topology has a central hub?
items.why: It's Star. “Bus” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Router. “Switch” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In a full mesh of 4 nodes, how many direct links exist?
items.why: Exact answer: 6 links. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
